models___init___py.content:
# Keep this package marker so imports work.
from models.base_task import BaseTask
from models.bug_task import BugTask
from models.feature_task import FeatureTask

__all__ = ["BaseTask", "BugTask", "FeatureTask"]

reports_summary_py.content:
# Starter guidance: Add focused model checks. Follow the prompt and edit the requested code below.
def build_summary(tasks):
    lines = ["SMART TASK TRACKER"]
    lines.extend(task.summary() for task in tasks)
    open_count = sum(task.is_open() for task in tasks)
    lines.append(f"OPEN: {open_count} | DONE: {len(tasks) - open_count}")
    return "\n".join(lines)

data_tasks_csv.content:
kind,title,priority,detail,completed
bug,Fix login redirect,high,critical,false
feature,Add dark mode,medium,3,false
feature,Export CSV,low,5,true

main_py.content:
from models import BugTask, FeatureTask

bug = BugTask("Fix login", "high", "critical")
feature = FeatureTask("Polish UI", "low", 0)
assert bug.is_open() is True
assert feature.estimate_points == 1
print("model checks passed")

capstone-tests-and-polish-sketch0.title: Capstone polish: prove the tracker is ready to hand off
capstone-tests-and-polish-sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
Polish is not just prettier output. It is proof that the app still works after all the pieces come together.

Imagine handing off a volunteer schedule app. You would want a teammate to know:

- what files matter
- how to run it
- what output to expect
- which tests protect the important behavior

The Smart Task Tracker needs the same handoff care. Add focused assertions, generate the final report, and leave a clear README so another person can understand the project without reading every line first.

Polish means more than formatting. The final workspace should prove its validation rules, explain how to run the program, and leave the next developer a predictable file structure and test command.
